d: A chronic elevation of systemic arterial blood pressure, defined as sustained readings of ≥140 mmHg systolic or ≥90 mmHg diastolic; a major risk factor for stroke, myocardial infarction, heart failure, and chronic kidney disease.

A group of metabolic diseases characterized by chronic hyperglycemia resulting from defects in insulin secretion, insulin action, or both; classified into type 1, type 2, gestational, and other specific types based on etiology.
Proactive health services (vaccinations, screenings, counseling) aimed at preventing disease before it develops.
The co-occurrence of two or more distinct medical conditions in the same individual, either related or unrelated to the primary diagnosis.
Ongoing, coordinated care strategies to control long-lasting conditions and maintain quality of life.

A condition of excessive body fat accumulation, defined by a body mass index (BMI) of 30 or above, associated with elevated risk of type 2 diabetes, cardiovascular disease, hypertension, and certain cancers.
Acute infectious inflammation of the upper airways, including the nose, pharynx, and larynx; caused predominantly by viruses and among the most common reasons for outpatient medical visits.
Patient's voluntary agreement to a proposed medical intervention after receiving adequate information.
The primary symptom or concern that brings a patient to seek medical care, recorded at the start of every encounter.
A comprehensive record of a patient's past illnesses, surgeries, medications, allergies, and family health background.
The clinical process of weighing probabilities of similar conditions to isolate the most likely diagnosis from others sharing similar signs and symptoms.
A forecast or prediction of the probable course and eventual outcome of a disease, including likelihood of recovery or progression.
An abnormal immune response to an ordinarily harmless substance (allergen) such as pollen, food, or medication, producing symptoms ranging from mild irritation to anaphylaxis.
A written or electronic instruction from a licensed clinician specifying a medication, dose, route, and duration.
Subjective evidence of disease experienced and reported by the patient (e.g. pain, fatigue, nausea).
Objective evidence of disease detected by a clinician during examination (e.g. fever, swelling, abnormal heart sound).
Five core measurements assessed at each encounter: blood pressure, heart rate, respiratory rate, body temperature, and oxygen saturation.
Sudden-onset, short-duration condition of often intense severity; contrasted with chronic.
Death of myocardial tissue caused by prolonged ischemia, most commonly from acute occlusion of a coronary artery by thrombus; presents with chest pain, ECG changes, and elevated cardiac biomarkers.

A spectrum of conditions caused by acute reduction in coronary blood flow, encompassing unstable angina, non-ST-elevation myocardial infarction (NSTEMI), and ST-elevation myocardial infarction (STEMI).
Paroxysmal chest pain or discomfort caused by transient myocardial ischemia, typically triggered by exertion or stress.

Syndrome in which the heart cannot pump sufficient blood to meet the body's metabolic demands, leading to fluid accumulation, breathlessness, and fatigue; classified as systolic (reduced ejection fraction) or diastolic (preserved ejection fraction).
Any abnormality in cardiac rate or rhythm — too fast, too slow, or irregular — ranging from harmless to life-threatening.
A common supraventricular tachyarrhythmia characterized by rapid, uncoordinated, and chaotic electrical activation of the cardiac atria, causing irregular heartbeat and stroke risk.
Diagnostic test recording electrical activity of the heart; essential for detecting arrhythmias, MI, and conduction abnormalities.
Narrowing or blockage of the coronary arteries due to atherosclerosis, leading to reduced blood supply to the heart.

A chronic inflammatory vascular disease in which lipid-laden plaques accumulate within the intima of arterial walls, progressively narrowing the lumen and reducing vessel elasticity; the leading cause of coronary artery disease and stroke.
Sudden loss of neurological function due to an interruption of blood supply to the brain; classified as ischemic or hemorrhagic.
Ischemic stroke caused by blockage of a cerebral artery; accounts for ~85% of all strokes.

Stroke caused by rupture of a blood vessel within or around the brain, causing bleeding into brain tissue (intracerebral) or the subarachnoid space; accounts for ~15% of all strokes with high mortality.
Insufficient blood supply to a tissue or organ, causing oxygen deprivation and risk of cell death.
Brief episode of neurological dysfunction from temporary loss of blood flow to the brain, resolving within 24 hours; a major warning sign for stroke.
Sudden cessation of effective heart pumping, causing loss of consciousness and death within minutes without CPR.
Escape of blood from ruptured blood vessels, which may occur internally or externally; classified by source (arterial, venous, capillary) and location.
Subjective awareness of an abnormally fast, slow, or irregular heartbeat; often a presenting symptom of arrhythmia or anxiety.
The process of identifying a disease or condition based on symptoms, signs, history, and test results.
Any intervention aimed at curing, managing, or alleviating a disease or its symptoms.
Describing a condition that persists over a long period (generally three months or more) and typically requires ongoing monitoring and management.

The body's protective immune response to tissue injury, infection, or irritation, characterized by vasodilation, increased vascular permeability, and leukocyte infiltration; manifests clinically as redness, swelling, heat, and pain.
The establishment and replication of pathogenic microorganisms — including bacteria, viruses, fungi, or parasites — within a host, potentially causing tissue damage and disease.
Body temperature above 38°C (100.4°F), typically indicating infection or systemic inflammation.
Unpleasant sensory and emotional experience associated with actual or potential tissue damage, varying in type, duration, and intensity.
The removal of a tissue sample from a living patient for microscopic examination; used to establish or confirm a diagnosis, particularly for suspected malignancy.
Describing a neoplasm or condition that is non-malignant, does not infiltrate adjacent tissue, and lacks the capacity for distant spread; generally associated with a favorable clinical outcome.
Describing a tumour that invades surrounding tissue and can metastasize to other parts of the body.
Spread of cancer cells from the primary site to distant organs or tissues via blood or lymphatic system.
A break or crack in a bone caused by trauma, stress, or underlying bone disease such as osteoporosis.

Abnormal accumulation of fluid in the interstitial spaces of tissues, causing swelling; may be localized (e.g., limb swelling) or generalized, and results from increased capillary pressure, reduced plasma proteins, or lymphatic obstruction.
Unpleasant urge to vomit arising from the upper abdomen; a nonspecific symptom of many gastrointestinal and systemic conditions.
Subjective sensation of difficulty or discomfort in breathing; a cardinal symptom of cardiac and respiratory disease.
The process of rapidly assessing and categorizing patients by severity of injury or illness to allocate limited medical resources to those who will benefit most.
Progressive decline in memory, language, and reasoning severe enough to impair daily life; Alzheimer's disease is the most common cause.
Progressive neurodegenerative disease causing dementia through accumulation of amyloid plaques and tau tangles, resulting in irreversible cognitive decline.
Chronic neurological disorder characterized by recurrent, unprovoked seizures due to abnormal electrical brain activity.
A sudden, uncontrolled electrical disturbance in the brain causing changes in behavior, movement, sensation, or consciousness; a symptom rather than a diagnosis in itself.
Progressive neurodegenerative disorder causing tremor, rigidity, and bradykinesia due to loss of dopamine-producing neurons.
Autoimmune disease in which the immune system attacks the myelin sheath of nerve fibers, disrupting brain-body communication.

Pain in any region of the head; classified as primary (arising from no underlying condition, e.g. migraine, tension-type) or secondary (due to an underlying condition such as infection or intracranial pathology).
Recurrent moderate-to-severe headache, often unilateral and throbbing, frequently accompanied by nausea and light sensitivity.
Damage or dysfunction of peripheral nerves causing numbness, tingling, weakness, or pain, commonly due to diabetes.
Decline in memory, language, judgment, or other thinking skills, ranging from mild (MCI) to severe (dementia).
Broad specialty focused on the prevention, diagnosis, and treatment of adult diseases not requiring surgery.
Medical specialty dealing with diagnosis and treatment of mental, emotional, and behavioural disorders.
Specialty devoted to the health and medical care of infants, children, and adolescents up to age 18.
Combined specialty covering pregnancy and childbirth (obstetrics) and female reproductive health (gynecology).
Specialty that uses operative techniques to treat injuries, diseases, and deformities of the body.
Specialty concerned with conditions of the musculoskeletal system including bones, joints, ligaments, tendons, and muscles.
Specialty dealing with disorders of the nervous system, including the brain, spinal cord, and peripheral nerves.
Medical specialty focused on diseases and conditions of the heart and cardiovascular system, including diagnosis, treatment, and prevention of cardiac disorders.
Specialty dedicated to the study, diagnosis, and treatment of cancer.
Abnormal concentrations of lipids or lipoproteins in the blood, including elevated total cholesterol, LDL, triglycerides, or reduced HDL; a major modifiable risk factor for cardiovascular disease.
Formal process of directing a patient from a primary care provider to a specialist for further evaluation or treatment.
The concurrent use of multiple medications by a single patient, increasing interaction risks and adverse effects; commonly defined as five or more drugs simultaneously.
Administration of a vaccine to stimulate immunity and protect against specific infectious diseases.
A deficiency in circulating thyroid hormones leading to a generalized metabolic slowdown, causing fatigue, weight gain, and cold intolerance.
A systemic skeletal disorder characterized by low bone mass and bone fragility, increasing susceptibility to fractures especially in postmenopausal women and older adults.
Mood disorder characterized by persistent low mood and loss of interest lasting at least two weeks, impairing daily function; associated with changes in sleep, appetite, and energy.
Bacterial infection of the urinary system (bladder, urethra, kidneys); one of the most common infectious diagnoses.
Weight (kg) divided by height squared (m²), used as a screening tool for underweight, overweight, and obesity.
Medical care provided without hospital admission; the patient visits a clinic and returns home the same day.
Scheduled subsequent visit to monitor a patient's condition, treatment response, or recovery progress.
Testing of asymptomatic individuals to detect disease or risk factors early, before symptoms develop.
The degree to which a patient correctly follows the prescribed treatment plan, including medications and lifestyle changes.
Sleep disorder characterized by persistent difficulty falling or staying asleep at least three nights weekly for three or more months, causing daytime impairment.
Pain in the lumbar spine, classified as acute (<6 weeks), subacute (6–12 weeks), or chronic (>12 weeks); a leading cause of disability worldwide.
Group of mental disorders marked by excessive, persistent fear or worry that impairs daily functioning; includes generalized anxiety, panic disorder, and phobias.
Chronic condition in which gastric acid refluxes into the esophagus, causing heartburn and regurgitation; severe cases may progress to esophagitis or Barrett's esophagus.
Reduction in red blood cells or hemoglobin below normal, impairing oxygen delivery to tissues; common causes include iron deficiency, chronic disease, and hemolysis.
A non-invasive diagnostic procedure utilizing ultrasound waves to evaluate the real-time structure and function of the heart.
Minimally invasive procedure to open blocked coronary arteries, typically using a balloon and stent.
Percentage of blood pumped out of the left ventricle with each heartbeat; key measure of heart function (normal ≥55%).
Drug that inhibits the coagulation cascade to prevent or treat thrombosis; used for atrial fibrillation, venous thromboembolism, and other thromboembolic conditions.
Implantable electronic device that delivers electrical impulses to regulate abnormally slow or irregular heartbeats.
Syndrome in which the heart cannot maintain adequate cardiac output, causing fluid congestion in the lungs and tissues; manifests as dyspnea, edema, and fatigue.
Emergency procedure combining chest compressions and rescue breaths to maintain circulation and oxygenation during cardiac arrest.
Imaging technique using strong magnetic fields and radio waves to produce detailed cross-sectional images of organs, soft tissues, bones, and the nervous system.
Imaging technique using X-rays to generate cross-sectional images of internal structures; first-line modality for acute trauma, stroke, and chest and abdominal assessment.
Procedure inserting a needle into the lumbar subarachnoid space to collect cerebrospinal fluid for diagnosis or to administer intrathecal medication.
Acute inflammation of the protective membranes covering the brain and spinal cord, usually caused by bacterial or viral infection.
An illusory sensation of spinning of oneself or the environment due to vestibular pathology; distinct from non-specific dizziness.
Non-specific sensation of lightheadedness or unsteadiness without a true spinning quality; distinct from vertigo.
Group of diseases characterized by progressive loss of nerve cell structure or function, including Alzheimer's and Parkinson's.
Damage to the spinal cord resulting in loss of motor, sensory, or autonomic function below the level of injury.
Recording of electrical activity of the brain via scalp electrodes; primary diagnostic tool for epilepsy and seizure disorders.
Impairment of language ability caused by brain damage, affecting speaking, understanding, reading, or writing.
Involuntary rhythmic muscle contraction causing shaking of one or more body parts; a hallmark symptom of Parkinson's disease.
A specific situation or pre-existing clinical condition in which a drug, procedure, or intervention must not be used due to risk of harm.
A drug that relieves pain without causing loss of consciousness; includes NSAIDs, acetaminophen, and opioids.
A medication that reduces elevated body temperature (fever); commonly combined with analgesics (e.g. acetaminophen, ibuprofen).
Route of drug or fluid administration directly into a vein, allowing rapid onset; used for urgent treatments and hydration.
An unintended, harmful response occurring at normal therapeutic doses of a drug; distinct from overdose or medication error.
Action taken or medication administered to prevent the occurrence or development of disease before it manifests.
Drug administration into the fatty tissue just beneath the skin; slower absorption than IV; used for insulin, heparin.
Drug administration directly into muscle tissue, providing faster absorption than subcutaneous; used for vaccines and some antibiotics.
Drug delivery by placing medication under the tongue for rapid absorption through the oral mucosa into the bloodstream, bypassing first-pass metabolism.
The ability of a drug or treatment to produce the desired therapeutic effect under controlled conditions; distinguished from effectiveness, which refers to real-world performance.
Subspecialty dealing with disorders of the endocrine glands and hormones, including diabetes and thyroid disease.
Specialty focused on conditions affecting the skin, hair, nails, and mucous membranes.
Specialty dealing with the anatomy, physiology, and diseases of the eye.
Specialty dealing with diseases of the ear, nose, throat, and related structures of the head and neck.
Specialty focused on the urinary tract in both sexes and the male reproductive system.
Specialty using medical imaging (X-ray, CT, MRI, ultrasound) to diagnose and guide treatment of disease.
Specialty focused on the immediate diagnosis and treatment of acute illnesses and injuries that require urgent care.
Specialty responsible for administering anesthesia and managing pain before, during, and after surgical and procedural interventions.
Specialty that examines tissues, organs, and bodily fluids to diagnose disease, often via biopsy or autopsy.
Study of how drugs interact with biological systems; foundational to prescribing and understanding medication effects.
Subspecialty focused on the diagnosis and treatment of infections caused by bacteria, viruses, fungi, and parasites.
Healthcare profession focused on the diagnosis, prevention, and treatment of diseases of the teeth, gums, and mouth.
Specialty aimed at restoring function and quality of life in patients with physical impairments due to injury, illness, or disability.
Drug class blocking beta-adrenergic receptors, reducing heart rate and blood pressure; used for hypertension, angina, arrhythmia, and heart failure.
Blood tests (e.g. troponin, CK-MB) measuring proteins released by damaged heart muscle, used to diagnose MI.
The degree to which individuals can obtain, process, and understand health information and services needed to make informed decisions about their health.
Legal document stating a patient's wishes regarding medical treatment in the event they become unable to communicate.
The consistent and coordinated provision of healthcare to a patient over time and across different care settings, encompassing the ongoing relationship between patient and provider.
Specialized care focused on relieving symptoms and improving quality of life for patients with serious illness, regardless of prognosis or treatment intent.
Surgical specialty focused on reconstructing or altering the human body for aesthetic or functional restoration purposes.
Surgical specialty dealing with diseases and conditions of the organs within the thoracic cavity, including the lungs, esophagus, mediastinum, and great vessels.
Surgical specialty for treating conditions of the brain, spinal cord, and peripheral nervous system.
Subspecialty focused on autoimmune and inflammatory diseases of the joints, muscles, and connective tissue.
Subspecialty focused on diseases of the respiratory tract and lungs.
Subspecialty focused on kidney diseases, electrolyte disorders, hypertension, and renal replacement therapy.
Subspecialty focused on the digestive system including the stomach, intestines, liver, pancreas, and gallbladder.
Subspecialty dealing with diseases of the blood and blood-forming organs, including anemia, clotting disorders, and leukemia.
Specialty using radioactive substances to visualise and treat diseases, including PET and bone scans.
A temporary loss of consciousness and muscle tone caused by a transient drop in blood flow to the brain.
Sharp, stabbing chest pain that worsens with deep inspiration, coughing, or moving the thorax; indicates pleural inflammation.
Vomiting of blood from the upper gastrointestinal tract; appears as bright red blood or dark coffee-ground material depending on bleeding rate.
The coughing up of blood or blood-stained sputum originating from the bronchi, larynx, trachea, or lungs.
The passage of dark, black, tarry stools containing altered blood, indicative of upper gastrointestinal bleeding.
The passage of fresh, bright red blood per rectum, usually indicating acute lower gastrointestinal bleeding.
An unpleasant, irritating sensation of the skin that provokes the urge to scratch; may be localised or generalised.
A bluish discoloration of the skin and mucous membranes resulting from inadequate oxygenation of the blood.
A hollow, muscular tube connecting the oral and nasal cavities to the esophagus and larynx, serving as a common pathway for food and air.
The cartilaginous organ located at the top of the trachea that houses the vocal cords and controls airflow during breathing and phonation.
A large membranous tube reinforced by rings of cartilage, extending from the larynx to the bronchi, conducting air to and from the lungs.
The muscular tube that propels swallowed food and liquids from the pharynx down to the stomach via peristalsis.
The first part of the small intestine immediately beyond the stomach, where chyme mixes with bile and pancreatic enzymes.
The highly vascularized middle segment of the small intestine specialized primarily for nutrient absorption.
The final segment of the small intestine, primarily responsible for absorbing vitamin B12 and bile salts; connects to the large intestine at the ileocecal valve.
The master endocrine gland at the base of the brain that regulates growth, reproduction, and hormone secretion from other endocrine glands.
A primary lymphoid organ in the superior mediastinum where bone marrow-derived T-lymphocytes differentiate and mature.
A small, pear-shaped organ underneath the liver that stores and concentrates bile before releasing it into the duodenum during digestion.
A non-cancerous enlargement of the prostate gland common in aging men, leading to lower urinary tract symptoms such as weak stream and frequency.
The abnormal presence of air in the pleural cavity causing lung collapse; may be spontaneous, traumatic, or iatrogenic.
The presence or formation of calculi within the gallbladder cavity, which may cause biliary colic or progress to cholecystitis.
A progressive, poorly reversible lung disease characterized by chronic airflow limitation, typically driven by smoking.
A chronic systemic autoimmune disorder characterized by symmetric inflammatory arthritis, primarily targeting synovial joints.
Procedure using a flexible fiber-optic instrument (endoscope) inserted into the body to directly visualize and treat internal structures such as the gastrointestinal tract or airways.
A minimally invasive surgical technique performed in the abdomen or pelvis through small incisions utilizing a camera.
The placement of a flexible plastic tube into the trachea past the vocal cords to maintain an open airway and assist ventilation.
A clinical procedure where a needle or catheter is inserted into the peritoneal cavity to sample or drain ascitic fluid.
The clinical practice of listening to internal body sounds using a stethoscope; used to assess heart rhythm and murmurs, breath sounds, and bowel activity.
A physical examination technique in which the examiner applies systematic manual pressure to assess the size, consistency, tenderness, and position of body structures and organs.
A glycoprotein manufactured by the prostate gland; blood concentrations serve as a clinical marker for BPH or malignancy screening.
Acute or chronic inflammation of the gallbladder wall, typically secondary to cystic duct obstruction by gallstones.
Acute inflammation of the appendix presenting with right lower quadrant pain, nausea, and fever; treated surgically or with antibiotics in uncomplicated cases.
Inflammation of the pancreas, characterized by premature activation of pancreatic enzymes leading to autodigestion of the organ tissue.
Inflammation of the peritoneum, usually from bacterial contamination after visceral perforation; presents with diffuse abdominal pain, rigidity, and fever.
The formation of crystalline calculi within the renal pelvis or calyces, causing severe flank pain, hematuria, and urinary obstruction.
Gradual, progressive, and irreversible loss of renal function lasting for three months or longer.
Total, permanent failure of the kidneys, requiring long-term renal replacement therapy (dialysis or kidney transplantation).
A medical procedure that uses a dialyzer machine to filter toxins, waste products, and excess fluids from the patient's blood when the kidneys fail.
A method of removing waste and excess fluid from the blood using the patient's peritoneum as a semipermeable membrane across which fluids exchange.
Distension and dilation of the renal pelvis caused by obstruction of urine outflow, leading to potential kidney damage if untreated.
A disorder of the inner ear characterized by brief, recurrent episodes of spinning vertigo triggered by specific changes in head position.
An inflammation or bacterial/viral infection of the middle ear space, frequently presenting with fluid accumulation behind the tympanic membrane.
The perception of noise or ringing in the ears or head when no external sound is present; may be continuous or intermittent.
Bleeding originating from the interior of the nasal cavity, most commonly from the Kiesselbach plexus on the anterior nasal septum.
Inflammation of the paranasal sinuses, most commonly triggered by viral upper respiratory infection; secondary bacterial infection occurs in some cases.
A visual disorder where the eyes point in different directions and fail to remain aligned simultaneously under normal viewing conditions.
Progressive opacification or clouding of the natural crystalline lens of the eye, obstructing the transmission of light onto the retina.
A group of eye diseases characterized by progressive optic nerve damage and visual field loss, often associated with elevated intraocular pressure.
An involuntary, rapid, and rhythmic oscillation of the eyeballs, which may be horizontal, vertical, or rotatory.
Progressive deterioration of the macula (the central area of the retina), causing loss of central vision while peripheral vision remains intact.
Redness of the skin or mucous membranes caused by increased blood flow in superficial capillaries, associated with inflammation or infection.
Transient raised, itchy wheals (hives) caused by histamine release from mast cells; triggers include allergens, medications, and infections.
An acute, spreading bacterial infection of the deep dermis and subcutaneous tissue, presenting with redness, warmth, swelling, and pain.
A chronic, pruritic, inflammatory skin disease that typically begins in infancy or childhood and follows a relapsing course.
Chronic immune-mediated skin disorder causing well-demarcated, scaly erythematous plaques; commonly affects the scalp, elbows, and knees.
An abnormal dermal sensation (such as tingling, pricking, or numbness) experienced without any clear external sensory stimulus.
Severe radiating pain originating in the lower back and traveling down the path of the sciatic nerve through the buttock and posterior leg.
Form of inflammatory arthritis caused by urate crystal deposition in joints; classically presents with sudden, severe pain in the big toe.
Excessive outward curvature of the thoracic spine producing a rounded or hunched posture.
Excess thyroid hormone production causing a hypermetabolic state with weight loss, palpitations, heat intolerance, and tremor.
A metabolic disorder caused by prolonged systemic exposure to abnormally elevated levels of glucocorticoids (cortisol).
A rare disorder of water metabolism caused by a deficiency of vasopressin (ADH) secretion or renal resistance to it, causing polyuria and polydipsia.
Progressive microvascular complications of long-standing diabetes mellitus affecting the blood vessels of the retina.
An abnormal reduction in the number of platelets in peripheral blood, increasing bleeding risk and causing petechiae or easy bruising.
An increase in the number of circulating white blood cells above normal range, typically a response to infection or severe inflammation.
A chronic bone marrow disorder causing uncontrolled overproduction of red blood cells, increasing blood viscosity and risk of thrombosis and stroke.
Formation of a thrombus within the deep venous system, most commonly in the lower extremities; may propagate and embolize to the pulmonary circulation, causing pulmonary embolism.
The sudden blockage of a pulmonary artery branch by a blood clot, usually originating from deep vein thrombosis, causing acute respiratory distress.
A tear in the inner aortic wall allowing blood to split between vessel layers, creating a false lumen; a cardiovascular emergency with high mortality.
Surgical removal of the gallbladder, performed most commonly via laparoscopy; the primary treatment for symptomatic cholelithiasis and cholecystitis.
Surgical removal of the vermiform appendix; the standard treatment for acute appendicitis, performed by open or laparoscopic technique.
A procedure in which a needle or catheter is inserted through the chest wall into the pleural space to withdraw fluid or air for diagnostic analysis or therapeutic relief.
Surgical removal of the uterus; indications include uterine fibroids, endometriosis, abnormal uterine bleeding, and gynecologic malignancies; may be partial or total.
A specialized, low-dose X-ray imaging procedure used to visualize internal breast tissue structures for early detection of cancer.
An endoscopic examination evaluating the inner lining of the large intestine and rectum via a flexible video scope.
A fatty deposit or plaque that forms on the inner lining of an arterial wall, characteristic of atherosclerosis.
Abnormal enlargement of the heart, usually resulting from chronic hypertension or heart failure.
A cardiac arrhythmia characterized by a slow heart rate, typically fewer than 60 beats per minute in adults.
A cardiac arrhythmia characterized by a rapid heart rate, typically exceeding 100 beats per minute in adults at rest.
A sudden drop in blood pressure (typically >20 mmHg systolic or >10 mmHg diastolic) that occurs when transitioning to an upright position.
Inflammation of the pericardium, the fibroserous sac surrounding the heart, often presenting with chest pain that improves when leaning forward.
An infection of the endocardium, most commonly involving the heart valves, typically caused by bacteria entering the bloodstream.
Cramping pain and weakness in the legs induced by exercise and relieved by rest, caused by peripheral artery disease.
A localized, abnormal dilation or ballooning of a blood vessel wall, carrying a permanent risk of rupture.
Collapse or incomplete expansion of lung tissue, impairing gas exchange; common postoperatively and in patients with mucus plugging or compression.
A chronic, progressive respiratory disease characterized by permanent dilation and destruction of the bronchial walls.
An abnormal accumulation of fluid in the pleural cavity surrounding the lungs.
A collection of whole blood in the pleural cavity, usually resulting from trauma or chest surgery.
An accumulation of pus within a body cavity, most commonly the pleural space; typically resulting from pneumonia, thoracic surgery, or chest trauma.
Abnormally elevated carbon dioxide (CO2) levels in arterial blood (PaCO2 >45 mmHg), typically indicating respiratory failure or hypoventilation.
Abnormally low oxygen levels in arterial blood (PaO2 <80 mmHg or SpO2 <95%), indicating impaired respiratory or circulatory function.
A harsh, high-pitched, vibrating respiratory sound caused by turbulent airflow through a partially obstructed upper airway.
Chronic progressive disease in which the lung interstitium becomes scarred and thickened, impairing gas exchange; idiopathic form is most common.
An abnormal accumulation of protein-rich fluid within the peritoneal cavity.
Late-stage scarring (fibrosis) of the liver tissue caused by chronic liver disease, compromising structural liver functions.
Sensation of difficulty or impairment in the passage of solid or liquid food from the mouth to the stomach.
Inflammation or infection of small pouches (diverticula) that can form along the walls of the intestines.
A yellowish discoloration of the skin, sclera, and mucous membranes caused by hyperbilirubinemia.
A serious condition in which part of the intestine slides or telescopes into an adjacent part, obstructing blood supply.
A loop of intestine twisting around itself and the mesentery that supports it, resulting in mechanical bowel obstruction.
Any bleeding originating from the digestive tract, classified broadly into upper (UGIB) and lower (LGIB).
A chronic syndrome characterized by delayed gastric emptying of solids or liquids without mechanical obstruction.
An abnormal accumulation of cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) within the ventricles of the brain, increasing intracranial pressure.
Lack of voluntary coordination of muscle movements, frequently resulting from cerebellar dysfunction or nerve damage.
Intense, typically burning or stabbing pain radiating along the course of one or more specific nerves.
Motor speech disorder from neurological injury causing slurred, slow, or imprecise pronunciation despite intact language comprehension.
Any condition causing altered brain function, presenting with confusion or impaired consciousness; causes include metabolic, toxic, infectious, and structural factors.
Acute inflammation of the active brain parenchyma, typically caused by viral infection.
The presence of abnormal amounts of protein in the urine, often an early clinical indicator of renal disease.
The abnormal presence of red blood cells in the urine, categorized into gross (visible) and microscopic.
The production of abnormally small amounts of urine, usually defined as less than 400 or 500 mL per day in adults.
Non-passage of urine, clinically defined as less than 100 mL of urine output per day; indicates severe kidney failure.
Immunologically mediated inflammation of the glomeruli, the microscopic filtering units of the kidney.
A collection of clinical findings caused by kidney damage, including massive proteinuria, hypoalbuminemia, hyperlipidemia, and severe edema.
An inflammation of the kidney substance and pelvis, typically resulting from an ascending bacterial infection originating in the urinary tract.
Inflammation of the urinary bladder wall, most frequently caused by an ascending bacterial infection.
The involuntary leakage or loss of control of urine, heavily subcategorized into stress, urge, and overflow types.
Pain, burning, or discomfort experienced during the process of urination, typical of lower urinary tract infections.
Abnormally elevated blood glucose levels (fasting >126 mg/dL or random >200 mg/dL); a hallmark of diabetes mellitus and a risk factor for acute and chronic complications.
Abnormally low blood glucose levels (typically <70 mg/dL), causing autonomic symptoms (sweating, tremor) and neuroglycopenic symptoms (confusion, loss of consciousness).
A life-threatening, acute metabolic complication of diabetes mellitus characterized by severe hyperglycemia, metabolic acidosis, and high blood ketone levels.
An abnormal benign proliferation of glandular breast tissue in males, caused by an imbalance between estrogen and androgen action.
A chronic disorder caused by excessive production of growth hormone (GH) by a pituitary adenoma after the closure of epiphyseal growth plates.
The spontaneous, inappropriate secretion of breast milk unrelated to childbearing or nursing, often linked to hyperprolactinemia.

en_l: Excessive male-pattern hair growth in women
d: Excessive growth of thick, dark hair in females following a male-like distribution pattern, resulting from androgen excess.
Overactivity of the parathyroid glands resulting in excess production of parathyroid hormone (PTH), causing hypercalcemia.

en_l: Adrenal tumor causing high blood pressure spike
d: A rare, neuroendocrine tumor of the adrenal medulla or extra-adrenal chromaffin tissue that secretes catecholamines.
A cluster of metabolic risk factors—including abdominal obesity, insulin resistance, dyslipidemia, and hypertension—elevating cardiovascular risks.

en_l: Red, white, and platelet cell drop
d: A simultaneous reduction in red blood cells, white blood cells, and platelets, typically caused by bone marrow failure, nutritional deficiency, or malignancy.
An abnormally low concentration of neutrophils (a type of white blood cell) in the blood, severely raising bacterial infection risks.
Enlargement of one or more lymph nodes, occurring as a reactive response to localized or systemic infection, inflammation, or malignancy.
Pathological enlargement of the spleen, commonly resulting from portal hypertension, hematologic malignancies, or chronic infections.
Systemic coagulopathy triggered by an underlying condition, consuming clotting factors and platelets and causing simultaneous thrombosis and hemorrhage.
A malignant neoplasm arising from epithelial cells; the most prevalent category of cancer, encompassing tumors of the lung, breast, colon, prostate, and many other organs.
A malignant tumor arising from mesenchymal supportive or connective tissues, such as bone, cartilage, fat, or muscle.

en_l: Target body's immune system to fight cancer
d: A type of cancer treatment that stimulates or restores the body's natural immune system mechanisms to detect and fight malignant cells.
Additional cancer treatment given after the primary treatment (such as surgery) to lower the risk of recurrence.
Treatment (chemotherapy, radiation, or targeted therapy) given before surgery to reduce tumor size and improve resectability.
Absence of menstruation; classified as primary (menarche has never occurred) or secondary (previously regular menstruation has stopped).
Painful menstruation or severe uterine cramping during menstrual bleeding, causing functional impairment.
A benign non-cancerous tumor of the uterine smooth muscle tissue, extremely common in women of reproductive age.
The premature separation of a normally implanted placenta from the uterine wall prior to the second stage of labor.
Heavy blood loss exceeding 500 mL (vaginal) or 1000 mL (C-section) within 24 hours following childbirth.
A rapid, severe, and potentially life-threatening systemic hypersensitivity reaction causing airway occlusion and vascular collapse.
Describing a disease or condition that arises spontaneously with no identifiable or known cause.
An adverse condition, illness, or injury induced inadvertently by a physician, medical team, drug therapy, or diagnostic procedure.
Increase in organ or tissue size due to enlargement of individual cells; commonly seen in cardiac muscle responding to increased workload.
Increase in cell number within a tissue or organ causing enlargement; may be physiological or pathological (e.g. prostatic hyperplasia).
A life-threatening organ dysfunction caused by a dysregulated host response to infection; defined clinically by an acute change in SOFA score ≥2 points.

en_l: Inability to recognize objects or faces
d: The inability to process sensory information and recognize objects or persons despite intact sensory organs.

en_l: Lack of awareness of one's own illness
d: A neurological condition in which a person with a disability or illness is unaware of or denies their own impairment; commonly seen after stroke or in dementia.
A behavioral syndrome characterized by abnormal movements, immobility, mutism, or extreme unresponsiveness.
A movement disorder characterized by a subjective feeling of inner restlessness and a compelling need to move.
A neurological disorder featuring involuntary, repetitive facial movements caused by long-term neuroleptic drug use.
An abnormal, painful, or intolerant sensitivity of the eyes to light.
The gradual, progressive, and bilateral loss of hearing that occurs naturally with aging.
The perception of two images of a single object; commonly caused by extraocular muscle weakness.
Progressive joint disease caused by articular cartilage degradation and bone remodeling; the most common form of arthritis, causing pain, stiffness, and reduced mobility.

en_l: Chronic spinal arthritis that fuses bones
d: A chronic, progressive inflammatory arthritis affecting the sacroiliac joints and axial skeleton.
The degenerative loss of skeletal muscle mass, quality, and strength associated with aging.
Pain in one or more muscles; may be localized (due to trauma or overuse) or diffuse (as in viral illness, fibromyalgia, or statin myopathy).
Lateral curvature of the spine exceeding 10 degrees, typically presenting during adolescent growth spurts; may be idiopathic, congenital, or neuromuscular in origin.

en_l: Bone tissue death from lack of blood supply
d: The cellular death of bone components due to temporary or permanent interruption of blood supply.
Inflammation of a tendon and its protective fluid-filled sheath from repetitive strain.
The partial or complete absence or loss of hair from areas where it normally grows.

en_l: Tiny purple or red skin spots
d: Pinpoint, round, non-raised purplish-red spots caused by minor intradermal hemorrhage.

en_l: Purple skin blotches from internal bleeding
d: Purple or red skin discoloration from bleeding beneath the surface, which does not blanch with pressure; may indicate platelet disorders, vasculitis, or coagulopathy.
A subcutaneous spot of bleeding larger than 1 centimeter in diameter resulting from vascular leakage.
A rapidly progressive, life-threatening destructive infection of subcutaneous tissue and fascia.
A gynecological emergency involving rotation of the ovary, compromising arterial inflow.

en_l: Fainting from sudden blood pressure drop
d: A transient loss of consciousness mediated by an inappropriate reflex causing bradycardia and vasodilation.
Circulatory disorder in which atherosclerosis narrows arteries and reduces blood flow to the limbs; classically presents with intermittent claudication.

en_l: White fingers from cold or stress
d: Episodic digital vasospasm triggered by cold or stress, causing color changes in fingers or toes: white (ischemia), blue (cyanosis), then red (reperfusion).
Chronic swelling of tissues caused by an accumulation of lymphatic fluid due to obstruction.
A life-threatening compression of the heart due to rapid accumulation of fluid in the pericardial sac.

en_l: Stones in the main bile duct
d: The presence of one or more gallstones within the common bile duct, potentially causing biliary obstruction, jaundice, cholangitis, or pancreatitis.
Bacterial infection of the biliary tract caused by obstruction and stasis of bile, classically presenting with Charcot's triad: fever, jaundice, and right upper quadrant pain.
Dilated submucosal veins in the esophagus from portal hypertension; rupture causes potentially fatal upper gastrointestinal hemorrhage.

en_l: Stomach lining changing to look like bowel
d: The transformation of gastric mucosa into a cellular phenotype resembling intestinal mucosa.
Failure of intestinal peristalsis causing functional bowel obstruction; classified as paralytic or mechanical, causing distension, nausea, and obstipation.
Dyspnea that occurs when lying flat, forcing the individual to remain upright to breathe.
Life-threatening inflammatory lung injury causing widespread alveolar damage and severe hypoxemia; most commonly triggered by sepsis, pneumonia, or trauma.

en_l: Percentage of drug absorbed into blood
d: The fraction of an administered drug dose that reaches the systemic circulation unchanged.
An early, non-specific symptom indicating the onset of a disease before major signs appear.

en_l: Conditions left behind after a disease
d: A pathological condition resulting from a prior, acute disease or injury.
Metabolic syndrome of progressive weight loss, muscle wasting, and fatigue associated with advanced cancer, heart failure, and chronic infections.

en_l: Dangerously low blood pressure from blood infection
d: Life-threatening subset of sepsis with circulatory failure; defined by vasopressor need and lactate >2 mmol/L despite adequate fluid resuscitation.
Abnormally elevated potassium levels in the blood (>5.5 mEq/L), which can cause life-threatening cardiac arrhythmias including ventricular fibrillation.
Surgical creation of an opening in the trachea below the larynx to secure an airway.
Dome-shaped musculotendinous partition separating the thoracic and abdominal cavities; the principal muscle of inhalation.
One of the two large airways that branch from the trachea into each lung, further subdividing into smaller bronchioles to distribute air throughout lung tissue.
Microscopic air sacs at the terminal ends of the bronchioles where oxygen and carbon dioxide exchange between air and blood takes place across thin capillary walls.
Thin double-layered membrane lining the thoracic cavity and enveloping each lung; the space between layers (pleural space) allows frictionless respiratory movement.
A leaf-shaped cartilaginous structure at the base of the tongue that folds over the glottis during swallowing to prevent aspiration of food and liquid into the trachea.
The thick muscular middle layer of the heart wall composed of cardiac muscle cells (cardiomyocytes), responsible for the contractile force that pumps blood.
Fibroserous double-walled sac enclosing the heart; inner serous layers secrete fluid to reduce friction during cardiac contraction.
Either of the two upper heart chambers receiving venous blood; the right receives deoxygenated blood from the body, the left receives oxygenated blood from the lungs.
Either of the two lower heart chambers that eject blood: the right pumps to the lungs, the left pumps oxygenated blood to the body.
The largest artery in the body, arising from the left ventricle and distributing oxygenated blood throughout the systemic circulation.
One of two large veins returning deoxygenated blood to the right atrium; SVC drains the head and upper extremities, IVC drains the trunk and lower extremities.
The distal outlet of the stomach connecting to the duodenum, encircled by the pyloric sphincter; regulates the rate of gastric emptying into the small intestine.

en_l: Pouch at start of large intestine
d: Blind-ended pouch at the junction of the ileum and ascending colon; the ileocecal valve controls flow into the large intestine, and the appendix projects from it.
A narrow, blind-ended tube attached to the cecum containing dense lymphoid tissue.
Major portion of the large intestine from cecum to rectum; absorbs water and electrolytes and compacts fecal matter into stool.
The terminal segment of the large intestine, approximately 12–15 cm in length, connecting the sigmoid colon to the anal canal; stores formed stool until defecation.
Mixed gland behind the stomach: exocrine portion secretes digestive enzymes into the small intestine; endocrine islets secrete insulin and glucagon to regulate blood glucose.
Structural and functional unit of the kidney, consisting of a glomerulus and renal tubules; filters blood and produces urine; each kidney contains approximately one million nephrons.
A tuft of fenestrated capillaries enclosed within Bowman's capsule in the renal cortex; the site of plasma ultrafiltration and the first step in urine formation.
A paired muscular tube that transports urine from the renal pelvis to the urinary bladder via peristaltic contractions.
The tube that conveys urine from the bladder to the exterior of the body; in males, also serves as the ejaculatory duct.
The largest part of the brain, divided into two hemispheres and four lobes, responsible for higher functions including thought, memory, language, sensation, and voluntary movement.
The region of the brain located at the back of the skull responsible for coordinating voluntary movement, balance, and fine motor control.
Stalk connecting the cerebrum to the spinal cord, comprising the medulla, pons, and midbrain; controls vital functions including respiration and heart rate.
Three protective membranes surrounding the brain and spinal cord: dura mater (outer), arachnoid mater (middle), and pia mater (inner); inflammation constitutes meningitis.
Structure in the medial temporal lobe critical for forming new declarative memories; one of the first regions affected in Alzheimer's disease.
Cylindrical nerve tissue column in the vertebral canal transmitting motor signals from brain to body and sensory signals from body to brain.
A curved long bone connecting the sternum to the scapula, forming part of the shoulder girdle; the most frequently fractured bone in the upper extremity.
Flat triangular bone of the posterior shoulder girdle articulating with the clavicle and humerus; provides attachment for major upper limb muscles.
A flat bone in the anterior midline of the thorax consisting of the manubrium, body, and xiphoid process; articulates with the clavicles and costal cartilages of ribs 1–7.
The longest and strongest bone in the body, extending from the hip joint proximally to the knee joint distally; the only bone of the thigh.
A triangular sesamoid bone embedded in the quadriceps tendon, articulating with the distal femur; protects the knee joint and improves the mechanical advantage of the quadriceps.
Fibrocartilaginous structure between vertebral bodies with an outer annulus fibrosus and inner nucleus pulposus; absorbs spinal loads and allows flexible movement.
Band of dense fibrous tissue connecting bone to bone across a joint, providing stability and limiting excessive movement.
A cord of dense, regularly arranged collagen fibers that connects skeletal muscle to bone, transmitting the force of muscular contraction to produce movement at a joint.
The outermost, avascular skin layer providing a protective barrier against pathogens, UV radiation, and water loss.
Dense connective tissue layer beneath the epidermis containing collagen, elastin, blood vessels, nerve endings, hair follicles, and glands.
Transparent, dome-shaped outermost layer of the eye covering the iris and pupil; refracts light and protects the eye from foreign material.
Light-sensitive layer lining the inner back of the eye; contains photoreceptors (rods and cones) that convert light into neural signals transmitted via the optic nerve.
A fluid-filled spiral structure in the inner ear containing hair cells that convert sound vibrations into electrical nerve impulses transmitted to the brain via the auditory nerve.
Small, bean-shaped organs of the immune system distributed along lymphatic vessels that filter lymph fluid and contain immune cells to detect and fight infection and cancer.
A butterfly-shaped endocrine gland in the neck that produces thyroid hormones (T3 and T4) regulating metabolism, energy, growth, and body temperature.
Hollow, pear-shaped muscular organ in the female pelvis where a fetus develops; periodically sheds its inner lining to produce menstrual flow.

A walnut-sized male reproductive gland surrounding the urethra below the bladder that produces seminal fluid nourishing and transporting sperm; commonly affected by benign enlargement and cancer in older men.
Medical specialty combining obstetrics (pregnancy and childbirth) and gynecology (female reproductive system health).
Specialty focused on the diagnosis and management of life-threatening conditions requiring continuous organ support and monitoring.
Surgical specialty focused on diseases of the arterial, venous, and lymphatic systems, excluding the heart and brain.
Surgical specialty dealing with disorders of the colon, rectum, and anus, including cancer, inflammatory bowel disease, and hemorrhoids.
The branch of medicine concerned with the structure and function of the immune system, including the study of allergies, autoimmune diseases, and immunodeficiencies.
Complete loss of consciousness and sensation induced by anesthetic agents, allowing surgical procedures to be performed without pain or awareness.
Reversible loss of sensation in a specific body area by blocking nerve conduction, without affecting consciousness.
Administration of medication to reduce anxiety, induce calm, or produce a sleep-like state, ranging from minimal to deep sedation.
Injection of local anesthetic into the epidural space of the lumbar spine to block lower-body pain; used in labor analgesia and postoperative pain management.
The funnel-shaped cavity at the center of each kidney that collects urine draining from the renal calyces and channels it into the ureter for transport to the bladder.
Injection of local anesthetic around a specific nerve or nerve bundle to block sensation in a targeted area without affecting consciousness.
A drug-induced state of reduced consciousness allowing the patient to respond to verbal commands, used for minor procedures.
Injection of local anesthetic into the subarachnoid space of the lumbar spine, producing temporary loss of sensation and movement below the injection site.
Imaging technique using high-frequency sound waves to visualize internal organs and structures in real time; no ionizing radiation involved.
Imaging technique using ionizing radiation to visualize dense structures such as bones and detect pulmonary pathology; the most widely used diagnostic imaging modality.
A substance administered before imaging to enhance the visibility of blood vessels, organs, or lesions on CT, MRI, or X-ray.
Treatment using physical methods such as exercise, manual therapy, and modalities to restore function, reduce pain, and improve mobility after injury or illness.
Therapy focused on helping patients regain the ability to perform daily activities after injury, illness, or disability.
The extent of movement possible at a joint; measured to assess injury, monitor recovery, and guide rehabilitation exercises.
Therapeutic intervention addressing communication, speech, language, voice, and swallowing disorders caused by neurological or developmental conditions.
The use of cytotoxic chemical agents to kill or inhibit the growth of cancer cells, administered orally or intravenously, often causing systemic side effects.
A hollow, muscular organ that pumps blood throughout the body via rhythmic contractions; consists of four chambers — two atria and two ventricles.
Blood vessels that supply oxygenated blood directly to the heart muscle; blockage leads to angina or myocardial infarction.
One of four structures (mitral, tricuspid, aortic, pulmonary) that ensure unidirectional blood flow through the heart chambers.
The smallest blood vessels, forming networks between arterioles and venules where exchange of oxygen, nutrients, and waste occurs between blood and tissues.
A pair of spongy, air-filled organs in the chest that carry out gas exchange — absorbing oxygen and expelling carbon dioxide with each breath.
The hollow space inside the nose that warms, humidifies, and filters inhaled air before it reaches the lower respiratory tract.
Air-filled cavities in the skull bones surrounding the nasal cavity; commonly inflamed in sinusitis.
A muscular, J-shaped organ between the esophagus and small intestine that stores food, secretes gastric acid, and begins protein digestion.
The largest internal organ, located in the upper right abdomen; responsible for metabolism, detoxification, protein synthesis, and bile production.
A long, coiled tube (~6–7 m) between the stomach and large intestine consisting of the duodenum, jejunum, and ileum; the primary site of nutrient absorption.
The final section of the digestive tract (~1.5 m), consisting of the cecum, colon, and rectum; absorbs water and electrolytes and forms stool.
The S-shaped final segment of the colon connecting to the rectum; a common site for diverticulosis and colorectal cancer.
A pair of bean-shaped organs in the retroperitoneum that filter blood, regulate fluid and electrolyte balance, and produce urine.
A hollow, muscular organ in the pelvis that stores urine produced by the kidneys until it is expelled through the urethra.
The central organ of the nervous system enclosed within the skull; controls thought, memory, emotion, sensation, motor skills, and all bodily functions.
A bundle of axons enclosed in connective tissue sheaths that transmit electrical signals between the central nervous system and target organs, muscles, and sensory receptors.
The fundamental cellular unit of the nervous system; specialized cells that receive, integrate, and transmit information through electrical impulses and chemical synaptic signals.
Rigid connective tissue that forms the skeleton; provides structural support, protects organs, enables movement, and produces blood cells in the marrow.
Contractile tissue classified as skeletal (voluntary), cardiac, or smooth (involuntary); responsible for movement, posture, and organ function.
Firm but flexible connective tissue covering joint surfaces, forming the nose and ears, and cushioning between vertebrae; lacks blood vessels and heals slowly.
The long bone of the upper arm extending from the shoulder joint to the elbow; commonly fractured in falls.
The larger of the two lower leg bones; bears most of the body weight and forms the medial side of the ankle joint.
One of the 33 bones forming the vertebral column; protects the spinal cord and provides structural support for the trunk.
Paired triangular glands above each kidney; cortex produces cortisol, aldosterone, and androgens; medulla secretes epinephrine and norepinephrine during stress.
A fist-sized lymphoid organ in the upper left abdomen that filters blood, recycles red blood cells, and plays a role in immune response.
A pair of female reproductive glands that produce eggs (ova) and secrete the hormones estrogen and progesterone.
The lower, narrow portion of the uterus connecting to the vagina; subject to routine screening for cancer via Pap smear.
A pair of male reproductive glands in the scrotum that produce sperm and testosterone.
The pigmented, contractile circular membrane of the eye that controls pupil size, regulating the amount of light reaching the retina.
A transparent, biconvex structure behind the iris that focuses light onto the retina; becomes opaque in cataract.
A thin, cone-shaped membrane separating the outer ear canal from the middle ear; vibrates in response to sound waves and transmits them to the ossicles.
The region between the thorax and pelvis, containing the stomach, intestines, liver, pancreas, and kidneys.
Movement of a limb away from the midline of the body.
Movement of a limb toward the midline of the body.
A malignant tumour from glandular epithelial cells; the most common cancer type in lung, colon, breast, and prostate.
Connective tissue of fat cells serving as energy storage, insulation, and organ cushioning.
Relating to the adrenal cortex, which produces cortisol, aldosterone, and androgens.
Able to use both hands equally; relevant in neurological and occupational assessments.
Deliberate artificial rupture of the amniotic membranes using a sterile instrument to induce or accelerate labor.
Hormones including testosterone promoting male characteristics; produced in testes, ovaries, and adrenal glands.
Imaging using contrast dye and X-rays to visualise blood vessels and detect blockages or aneurysms.
Complete absence of oxygen to tissues; more severe than hypoxia, causing rapid cell death.
A drug that kills or inhibits bacterial growth; classified as bactericidal or bacteriostatic.
Narrowing of the aortic valve or aorta, obstructing left ventricular outflow.
Hardening and loss of elasticity of arterial walls; broader than atherosclerosis.
Inflammation of the brachial artery; associated with systemic vasculitis or trauma.
Inflammation of one or more joints causing pain, swelling, and stiffness; encompasses over 100 conditions.
Aspiration of fluid from a joint space for diagnosis or pain relief.
A fiber-optic instrument inserted through a small incision into a joint to visualize its internal structures for diagnosis and surgical treatment.
A minimally invasive procedure using an arthroscope to diagnose and treat joint problems.
The major vein draining the arm and shoulder; commonly accessed for central venous catheter placement.
An agent that kills bacteria; includes antibiotics, disinfectants, and antiseptics.
A two-headed muscle on the front of the upper arm responsible for elbow flexion and forearm supination.
Involuntary forceful contraction of the eyelid muscles; may be benign or a neurological sign.
A short, broad skull from premature coronal suture fusion; seen in genetic syndromes and positional deformity.
Abnormally slow breathing (fewer than 12 breaths per minute); may indicate CNS depression or opioid overdose.
Inflammation of the bronchial tubes; acute (usually viral) or chronic (>3 months/year for 2+ years).
The principal cheek muscle; compresses cheeks during chewing and is important in speech and swallowing.
Originating from the heart; cardiogenic shock is life-threatening cardiac pump failure.
Relating to the eight small wrist bones connecting the forearm to the hand.
A narrow wrist passageway for the median nerve and flexor tendons; compression causes numbness and weakness.
Pain in any part of the head; primary (migraine, tension) or secondary (infection, hypertension, intracranial pathology).
Direction or development from head toward feet; used in growth patterns and neurological development.
Relating to the angle between the cerebellum and pons; a common site for acoustic neuromas.
Clear fluid circulating around the brain and spinal cord for cushioning and waste removal; sampled via lumbar puncture.
An immature cell producing cartilage matrix; important in bone development and repair.
Thread-like DNA-protein structures; humans have 46 chromosomes carrying genetic information.
A surgical procedure creating an opening (stoma) in the abdominal wall through which a portion of the colon is brought, diverting fecal material into an external pouch.
An abnormal colon-bladder connection causing recurrent UTIs and air in urine.
Relating to the opposite side of the body; used in neurological deficits and anatomical descriptions.
Inflammation of costal cartilages at the sternum, causing chest pain mimicking cardiac pain.
A surgical procedure in which a section of the skull is temporarily removed to access the brain for tumor resection, hematoma evacuation, or aneurysm repair.
A tubular instrument with camera and light to examine the bladder and urethra.
Toxic to living cells; cytotoxic drugs destroy rapidly dividing cancer cells.
Radiographic examination of the duodenum using contrast medium.
Describing a person who has lost all or most of their natural teeth; requires specialized dental management and specific considerations for airway management under anesthesia.
The device recording heart electrical activity to produce the ECG tracing.
Recording cardiac electrical activity via skin electrodes; diagnoses arrhythmias, MI, and conduction abnormalities.
The smooth membrane lining heart chambers and valves; inflamed in endocarditis.
A tubular instrument with camera and light for visualising hollow organs or body cavities.
Inflammation of both small intestine and colon; causes include infection and Crohn's disease.
Mature red blood cells lacking a nucleus, containing hemoglobin that transports oxygen from the lungs to tissues and returns carbon dioxide for exhalation.
Steroid hormones from the ovaries regulating the female reproductive system, bone density, and cardiovascular health.
Forearm muscles flexing the fingers; tested in hand and nerve injury assessment.
Relating to both frontal and parietal bones or lobes of the skull or brain.
Inflammation of the stomach lining from H. pylori, NSAIDs, or alcohol; causes epigastric pain and nausea.
Herniation or protrusion of the stomach through a structural defect.
Knees angling inward; normal in young children, pathological if persistent or severe.
Legs bowing outward at the knees; normal in infants under age 2, pathological if persistent.
Glucose intolerance first diagnosed during pregnancy; increases complication risk and predisposes to type 2 diabetes.
A portable device measuring blood glucose from a small sample; essential for diabetes monitoring.
The metabolic breakdown of glucose into pyruvate to produce ATP; the first step in cellular respiration.
Surgical removal of approximately half the stomach for peptic ulcer disease or gastric cancer.
Weakness affecting one side of the body, typically from stroke or brain injury.
Complete paralysis of one side of the body from damage to the motor cortex or brainstem.
The physiological process stopping bleeding through vasoconstriction, platelet aggregation, and coagulation.
Abnormal liver enlargement; caused by hepatitis, cirrhosis, heart failure, or malignancy.
A chronic painful skin condition causing abscesses and scarring in sweat gland areas (hidradenitis suppurativa).
Blockage of the intestine preventing passage of contents; may be mechanical (adhesions, hernias, tumors) or functional (ileus); can cause ischemia requiring urgent management.
Relating to the same side of the body; used in neurological and anatomical descriptions.
Relating to both lips and teeth; sounds produced with upper teeth and lower lip (e.g. /f/, /v/).
The almond-shaped gland in the upper outer orbit producing tears to lubricate and protect the eye.
A surgical incision into the abdominal cavity for diagnostic or therapeutic purposes.
Sudden involuntary laryngeal muscle contraction causing vocal cord closure; a medical emergency.
The first day of the most recent menstrual period; used to calculate gestational age and due date.
White blood cells forming the cellular immune defense; classified as granulocytes (neutrophils, eosinophils, basophils) and agranulocytes (lymphocytes, monocytes).
Relating to both tongue and gum; used in dental anatomy for structures at the tongue-gingiva interface.
A procedure using shock waves or laser to break up kidney or gallstones.
Abnormal tongue enlargement; associated with Down syndrome, acromegaly, and hypothyroidism.
A condition from insufficient, excessive, or imbalanced nutrient intake.
A malignant tumour from melanocytes; the most dangerous skin cancer with high metastatic potential.
One of two C-shaped fibrocartilage structures in the knee; commonly torn in sports injuries.
The sum of all chemical reactions maintaining life; includes catabolism and anabolism.
Relating to the five long bones of the hand connecting wrist to finger bones.
Inflammation and ulceration of mucous membranes; a common painful side effect of chemotherapy.
Relating to the muscles and skeleton together; provides structure, movement, and organ protection.
The study of fungi including medically important species causing candidiasis and aspergillosis.
An immature precursor cell in the bone marrow that develops into granulocytes; abnormally elevated counts are characteristic of acute myeloid leukemia.
Imaging using contrast dye injected into the spinal canal to visualise spinal cord and nerve roots.
A malignant neoplasm of plasma cells in the bone marrow; multiple myeloma is the most common form, causing bone destruction, anemia, hypercalcemia, and impaired immunity.
Fungal infection of the tympanic membrane; associated with prolonged antibiotic use.
The upper pharynx behind the nasal cavity; contains adenoids and Eustachian tube openings.
Relating to the first 28 days of life; critical for identifying congenital conditions.
Surgical removal of a kidney for renal cancer, severe trauma, or living donation.
The formation of new neurons; primarily during fetal development with limited adult continuation.
Having normal blood pressure (systolic <120 and diastolic <80 mmHg).
Relating to eye movement; CN III controls most eye movements, pupil constriction, and eyelid elevation.
A mutated gene promoting uncontrolled cell division; derived from normal proto-oncogenes.
The middle pharynx with tonsils, soft palate, and tongue base; a site for tonsillitis and HPV-related cancer.
A bony projection at joint edges from cartilage degeneration; can cause pain and restrict movement.
Ear canal discharge; may be watery (CSF leak), purulent (infection), or bloody (trauma).
A handheld instrument with light and magnifying lens for examining the ear canal and tympanic membrane.
Complete loss of voluntary muscle function from nervous system damage; may be temporary or permanent.
A circumferential compression device applied in trauma to stabilize an unstable pelvic fracture, reduce pelvic volume, and control life-threatening hemorrhage.
Needle aspiration of pericardial fluid; performed emergently for cardiac tamponade.
Serious gum and bone infection from plaque bacteria; a leading cause of tooth loss.
Dense fibrous membrane covering bone surfaces; contains blood vessels and osteoblasts for bone repair.
Surrounding blood vessels; perivascular inflammation is seen in vasculitis and autoimmune diseases.
A graphic recording of heart sounds and murmurs for cardiac auscultation documentation.
Obliteration of the pleural space to prevent recurrent effusions or pneumothorax.
Abnormally excessive thirst; a classic symptom of diabetes mellitus and diabetes insipidus.
Abnormally large urine production (>3 litres/day); a cardinal symptom of diabetes mellitus and insipidus.
Located behind the ear; used for cochlear implant incisions and lymph node assessment.
Relating to the period following surgery; includes pain management, wound care, and monitoring.
Occurring after childbirth; the postpartum period typically refers to the six weeks following delivery.
Occurring before birth; prenatal care includes check-ups and screening during pregnancy.
Relating to the period before surgery; includes history, examination, investigations, and consent.
A dental specialist focused on restoring and replacing teeth using dentures, bridges, and implants.
Extension forward beyond a surface; in dentistry refers to jaw position, in neurology to disc herniation.
A nuclear medicine scan using a tracer to assess kidney blood flow, function, and drainage.
Surgical reshaping of the nose for cosmetic or functional purposes.
Excessive nasal discharge; may be clear (viral URI, allergy, CSF leak) or purulent (bacterial sinusitis).
Episodes of intense, uncontrollable shivering accompanied by a rapid rise in body temperature; a classic sign of bacteremia or sepsis.
An endoscopic instrument for examining the sigmoid colon and rectum; used for screening and polyp removal.
A wire loop device deployed through an endoscope to grasp and remove polyps, tumors, or foreign bodies by electrosurgical excision or mechanical avulsion.
Relating to sensory signals from the body including touch, pain, temperature, and proprioception.
A device measuring volume and speed of air inhaled and exhaled; used in pulmonary function testing.
Nerves supplying the viscera; carry sympathetic fibres regulating gut motility and vascular tone.
Surgical removal of the spleen; indicated for traumatic rupture, hematologic disorders such as immune thrombocytopenia, or splenic malignancy.
Degenerative spinal changes including osteophyte formation and disc degeneration; a common cause of back pain.
A medical instrument used to listen to internal body sounds, particularly heart and lungs.
Located or administered under the tongue; allows rapid bloodstream absorption (e.g. GTN for angina).
Located above the gum line; supragingival tartar is removed during routine dental cleaning.
Exhibiting symptoms of disease; contrasted with asymptomatic.
Abnormally rapid breathing (above 20 breaths per minute); a sign of respiratory distress or metabolic acidosis.
Surgical incision into the chest wall to access the thoracic cavity.
Surgical or catheter-based removal of a blood clot; used urgently in stroke, DVT, and PE.
Excess thyroid hormone action; presents with weight loss, palpitations, tremor, and heat intolerance.
A generalised seizure with tonic (stiffening) then clonic (jerking) phases; formerly called grand mal.
The study of body surface features and their relationships to underlying structures.
The study of adverse effects of chemicals and drugs; clinical toxicology manages poisoning and overdose.
Administered or occurring through the skin; patches allow sustained drug absorption.
A three-headed muscle on the back of the upper arm responsible for elbow extension; tests radial nerve (C7).
A sudden, sharp, brief pain; describes fleeting musculoskeletal or nerve-related discomfort.
A brief involuntary muscle contraction; may be benign or a sign of neuromuscular disease.
Calcification and scarring of the tympanic membrane from recurrent otitis media; can cause hearing loss.
Relating to the ulna, the medial forearm bone; the ulnar nerve runs alongside it.
The flexible cord connecting the fetus to the placenta; contains two arteries and one vein.
Enlarged, twisted veins usually in the legs; caused by weakened venous valves.
Narrowing of blood vessels by smooth muscle contraction; raises blood pressure and reduces peripheral flow.
Widening of blood vessels by smooth muscle relaxation; lowers blood pressure and increases blood flow.
Sudden, reversible contraction of a blood vessel reducing its lumen; clinically significant in Raynaud phenomenon and as a complication of subarachnoid hemorrhage.
Slowing or stagnation of venous blood flow; predisposes to deep vein thrombosis.
A machine mechanically assisting or replacing spontaneous breathing in patients who cannot breathe adequately.
Sensory receptors within internal organs detecting pressure, stretch, pain, and chemical changes.
Consciously controlled movement via the motor cortex; impaired in upper motor neuron lesions.
A pulse difficult to palpate from reduced stroke volume or vasoconstriction; indicates shock or cardiac compromise.
Yellow discoloration of CSF from hemoglobin breakdown; key finding confirming subarachnoid hemorrhage when CT is negative.
Surgical reshaping and tightening of the abdominal wall; removes excess skin and fat.
Surgical removal of one or both adrenal glands; performed for tumors or hyperfunction.
Sampling of amniotic fluid by needle puncture for fetal genetic or developmental testing.
X-ray image of blood vessels obtained after injection of contrast dye.
A minimally invasive endovascular procedure that widens narrowed or obstructed blood vessels, typically using a balloon catheter with or without stent placement.
Radiographic imaging of the aorta using injected contrast dye.
X-ray imaging of arteries using contrast dye to detect blockages or abnormalities.
Direct visual examination of the airways using a flexible fiber-optic scope.
Surgical removal of one or more carpal (wrist) bones.
Radiographic imaging of the bile ducts using contrast dye.
Surgical incision into the common bile duct to remove stones or relieve obstruction.
Visual examination of the bladder and urethra using a cystoscope.
Endoscopic examination of the duodenum.
Endoscopic visual examination of the small intestine.
Direct visual examination of the esophagus using an endoscope.
Surgical removal of fascia tissue; used to treat Dupuytren's contracture or compartment syndrome.
Surgical removal of all or part of the stomach; performed for cancer or severe ulcer disease.
Surgical removal of gum tissue to treat severe periodontal disease.
Direct visual examination of the uterine cavity using a hysteroscope.
Surgical creation of an opening from the ileum to the abdominal wall for waste elimination.
Minimally invasive procedure to restore vertebral height and relieve pain from compression fractures.
Surgical reshaping of the labia for functional or cosmetic purposes.
Visual examination of the larynx using a laryngoscope.
Low-dose X-ray image of breast tissue used for screening or diagnosis of breast cancer.
Surgical reshaping or reconstruction of the breast.
Surgical incision of the eardrum to drain fluid from the middle ear.
Surgical crushing of a nerve to relieve chronic or intractable pain.
Surgical removal of one or both testes; performed for testicular cancer or hormone therapy.
Surgical removal of an ovary; performed for cysts, tumors, or torsion.
Imaging of the pancreatic ducts, typically via ERCP.
The puncture of a vein to collect blood for laboratory analysis, therapeutic bloodletting (as in polycythemia vera), or blood donation.
Visual examination of the rectum and anus using a rigid scope.
Radiographic imaging of the renal pelvis and urinary tract using contrast dye.
Surgical incision of the pyloric muscle to relieve infantile pyloric stenosis.
Surgical repair of a deviated nasal septum to improve airflow.
Visual examination of the sigmoid colon and rectum using a flexible scope.
Surgical incision into a paranasal sinus to drain infection or remove obstruction.
Surgical incision through the sternum to access the thoracic cavity.
Auscultatory examination of the chest using a stethoscope.
Surgical removal of all or part of the thyroid gland.
Imaging technique that generates cross-sectional images of the body.
Surgical incision through the tracheal wall creating an airway; performed for obstruction, prolonged mechanical ventilation, or failed intubation.
Surgical repair of the tympanic membrane.
Visual examination of the ureter using a ureteroscope.
Surgical repair of the urethra for stricture or injury.
Surgical severing of the vas deferens for permanent male sterilization.
Radiographic or nuclear imaging of the cardiac or cerebral ventricles.
Surgical creation of an opening from the bladder to the skin surface.
A persistent, irrational fear of heights that triggers significant anxiety or avoidance behavior; classified as a specific phobia.
Inflammation of a lymph node or gland, typically in response to regional bacterial or viral infection; presents with local tenderness and swelling.
Anxiety disorder characterized by fear of open or crowded spaces from which escape is difficult.
A slightly movable joint connected by fibrocartilage; e.g., pubic symphysis.
Inability to sweat normally, impairing the body's ability to regulate temperature.
Stiffening and immobility of a joint due to fusion of bones or fibrous tissue.
Narrowing of the aortic valve opening, obstructing blood flow from the left ventricle.
Chronic inflammation of the eyelid margins, often caused by bacterial infection or seborrhea.
Drooping of the upper eyelid due to muscle weakness or nerve damage.
Inflammation of the cerebellum, often following viral infection.
Intense anxiety triggered by enclosed or confined spaces.
Inflammation of the colon; causes include infection, IBD, and ischemia.
Inflammation of the cornea; also called keratitis.
Premature fusion of one or more cranial sutures in infants, restricting brain growth.
Infection and inflammation of the lacrimal sac, causing tearing and discharge.
Inflammation of the skin causing redness, itching, and rash; multiple subtypes exist.
Inflammation of the duodenum, often caused by H. pylori or NSAIDs.
Inflammation of the small intestine, typically from infection or Crohn's disease.
Acute inflammation of the epiglottis, a life-threatening airway emergency.
Inflammation of the esophageal lining, most commonly from acid reflux or infection.
Inflammation of the fascia; includes plantar fasciitis and necrotizing fasciitis.
Chronic condition characterized by widespread musculoskeletal pain, fatigue, and cognitive disturbance.
Excessive formation of fibrous connective tissue as a reparative response to injury.
Inflammation of the gingiva (gums), the earliest stage of periodontal disease.
Scarring and hardening of the glomeruli, leading to progressive kidney failure.
Inflammation of the tongue causing swelling, color change, and altered texture.
Inflammation of the liver; causes include viral infection (A, B, C), alcohol, and autoimmune disease.
