Excessive sweating beyond what is needed for thermoregulation.
Inflammation of the ileum; a common manifestation of Crohn's disease.
Inflammation of the jejunum, often from Crohn's disease or infection.
Simultaneous inflammation of the jejunum and ileum.
Inflammation of the larynx causing hoarseness or voice loss.
Inflammation of the tympanic membrane, often accompanying otitis media.
Death of body tissue due to injury, infection, or impaired blood supply.
Abnormal downward displacement of the kidney when standing.
Fungal infection of the nails, causing thickening, discoloration, and brittleness.
Inflammation of one or both testes, typically from viral or bacterial infection.
Infection of bone, most commonly caused by Staphylococcus aureus.
Inflammation of the ear; classified as otitis externa (outer) or otitis media (middle).
Inflammation of the pharynx causing sore throat; usually viral, sometimes streptococcal.
Inflammation of a vein wall, often associated with clot formation.
Inflammation of the pleural lining causing sharp chest pain that worsens with breathing.
Viral infection affecting the gray matter of the spinal cord, potentially causing paralysis.
Inflammation of the rectal lining; causes include IBD, infection, and radiation.
Inflammation of the prostate gland; can be acute bacterial, chronic bacterial, or non-bacterial.
Severe mental disorder involving loss of contact with reality, hallucinations, or delusions.
Narrowing of the pyloric outlet obstructing gastric emptying; common in male infants.
Impulse control disorder characterized by compulsive fire-setting.
Inflammation of the nasal mucosa causing congestion, runny nose, and sneezing.
Multisystem inflammatory disease characterized by non-caseating granulomas.
Inflammation of the sigmoid colon.
Pain along the spine; may arise from muscle, nerve, or vertebral pathology.
Inflammation of one or more vertebrae; as in ankylosing spondylitis.
Inflammation of the synovial membrane lining a joint, causing pain and swelling.
Inflammation of a tendon, typically from overuse or repetitive strain.
Formation of a blood clot within a blood vessel, obstructing flow.
Inflammation of the tonsils, most often caused by viral or streptococcal infection.
Inflammation of the trachea, often bacterial; can cause croup-like symptoms.
Impulse control disorder involving compulsive urge to pull out one's own hair.
Inflammation of the urethra, commonly caused by sexually transmitted infections.
Formation of a blood clot within a vein; includes DVT and superficial thrombophlebitis.
Benign tumor arising from the vestibulocochlear nerve, causing hearing loss and tinnitus.
Benign epithelial tumor arising from glandular tissue; can occur in thyroid, colon, pituitary.
Catecholamine hormone secreted by the adrenal medulla; triggers the fight-or-flight response.
Habitual swallowing of excess air, leading to bloating, belching, and abdominal discomfort.
Intestinal infection caused by Entamoeba histolytica; may cause dysentery or liver abscess.
Age-related gradual decline in testosterone levels in men; also called late-onset hypogonadism.
Loss of sensation induced pharmacologically for surgical or diagnostic procedures.
A permanent, abnormal focal dilation of the aorta to more than 1.5 times its normal diameter; most common in the infrarenal abdominal aorta; rupture carries very high mortality.
Complete cessation of breathing; may be obstructive, central, or mixed.
Surgical fusion of a joint to eliminate movement and relieve pain.
Wasting or reduction in size of a tissue or organ due to disuse, malnutrition, or disease.
Standardized testing of hearing sensitivity across frequencies.
Pertaining to a condition in which the immune system attacks the body's own tissues.
Post-mortem examination of a body to determine cause of death or study disease.
Presence of viable bacteria in the bloodstream; may lead to sepsis.
Radiation therapy delivered by placing a radioactive source directly inside or near a tumor.
Slowness of voluntary movement; a cardinal feature of Parkinson's disease.
Narrowing of the bronchial airways due to smooth muscle contraction; occurs in asthma.
Widening of the bronchial airways to improve airflow; achieved by bronchodilator drugs.
Sudden involuntary contraction of bronchial smooth muscle causing wheezing and dyspnea.
Physician specializing in the diagnosis and treatment of cardiovascular disease.
Restoration of normal cardiac rhythm using synchronized electrical shock or medication.
Compression of the median nerve at the wrist causing numbness, tingling, and weakness.
Group of permanent movement and posture disorders caused by brain damage before or during birth.
Benign tumor composed of mature cartilage tissue.
Softening and deterioration of cartilage, most commonly under the kneecap.
The process by which blood forms clots to stop bleeding.
Persistent pain in the coccyx (tailbone) region, often following trauma.
Surgical replacement of a damaged or diseased cornea with donor tissue.
Small signaling protein secreted by immune cells that regulates immune responses.
Removal of dead, damaged, or infected tissue to promote healing of a wound.
Deficit of body water causing electrolyte imbalance and reduced physiological function.
Healthcare professional specializing in the prevention, diagnosis, and treatment of oral disease.
Physician specializing in diseases of the skin, hair, and nails.
Medical procedure that filters waste, excess fluid, and toxins from blood when kidneys fail.
Frequent passage of loose or watery stools; a symptom of many gastrointestinal conditions.
Impaired or involuntary abnormal movements; a complication of long-term levodopa therapy.
Impaired ability to produce or comprehend language due to brain damage.
Difficulty or abnormality in voice production.
Chronic lung disease with permanent alveolar destruction, reducing gas exchange surface.
The inner mucous membrane lining the uterus; shed during menstruation.
Hormone produced by the kidneys stimulating red blood cell production in bone marrow.
A weaker form of estrogen measured in pregnancy screening for fetal abnormalities.
Pertaining to glands that secrete substances through ducts onto body surfaces.
Abnormal protrusion of one or both eyeballs; associated with Graves' disease.
Pancreatic hormone that raises blood glucose by stimulating hepatic glycogen breakdown.
Presence of glucose in the urine; a sign of uncontrolled diabetes mellitus.
Iron-containing protein in red blood cells that carries oxygen from lungs to tissues.
Destruction of red blood cells with release of hemoglobin into circulation.
Malignant tumor of hepatocytes; also called hepatocellular carcinoma.
Causing damage or injury to liver cells.
Surgical repair of a hernia by suturing the defect in the abdominal wall.
Severe, uncontrollable vomiting; hyperemesis gravidarum occurs in early pregnancy.
Abnormally increased sensitivity to sensory stimuli including touch, pain, or temperature.
Refractive error in which distant objects are seen more clearly than near objects.
Abnormally increased muscle tone or tension.
Abnormally low potassium concentration in the blood; causes muscle weakness and arrhythmia.
Dangerously low core body temperature below 35°C (95°F).
State of impaired immune function leaving the body susceptible to infection.
Malignant proliferation of white blood cell precursors in bone marrow and blood.
Abnormally low white blood cell count, increasing susceptibility to infection.
Benign soft tissue tumor composed of mature fat cells; usually painless.
Endoscopic crushing of a bladder stone followed by irrigation to remove fragments.
Cancer of lymphatic tissue; includes Hodgkin and non-Hodgkin subtypes.
Flat, discolored skin lesion less than 1 cm in diameter; not raised or palpable.
Abnormal enlargement of the heart; also called cardiomegaly.
Coughing up black or dark-colored sputum; seen in coal workers' pneumoconiosis.
Usually benign, slow-growing tumor arising from the meninges.
Protrusion of the meninges through a defect in the skull or spine.
Permanent cessation of menstruation marking the end of female reproductive capacity.
Abnormally heavy or prolonged menstrual bleeding.
Abnormally small head circumference due to impaired brain development.
Disease or dysfunction of the spinal cord.
Oxygen-binding protein in muscle tissue; released into blood after muscle injury.
Disease of the muscle characterized by weakness and wasting.
Refractive error in which near objects are seen clearly but distant objects are blurred.
Chronic neurological disorder causing uncontrollable episodes of daytime sleepiness.
Any new and abnormal tissue growth; may be benign or malignant.
Surgical fixation of a prolapsed kidney to the posterior abdominal wall.
Capable of causing damage to kidney tissue.
Malignant tumor arising from immature nerve cells; most common in children under 5.
Physician specializing in disorders of the nervous system.
The most abundant white blood cell; a key component of innate immune defense.
Abnormally low sperm count in semen; a common cause of male infertility.
Instrument used to examine the interior of the eye, including the retina and optic disc.
Surgical fixation of an undescended testicle into the scrotum.
Dental specialty focused on correcting misaligned teeth and jaws.
Softening of bones due to impaired mineralization, often from vitamin D deficiency.
Fluid-filled sac on or within the ovary; most are benign and resolve spontaneously.
Swelling of the optic disc caused by raised intracranial pressure.
Paralysis of the lower body and legs due to spinal cord injury or disease.
Four small endocrine glands adjacent to the thyroid that regulate calcium and phosphate.
Infection of the female upper reproductive tract; a complication of STIs.
Licensed healthcare professional who prepares, dispenses, and counsels on medications.
Therapeutic treatment using physical methods to restore movement and function.
Sharp pain in the chest or abdomen from spasm of intercostal or pleural muscles.
Inflammatory infection of the lung parenchyma caused by bacteria, viruses, or fungi.
Group of metabolic disorders caused by enzyme defects in heme biosynthesis.
Abnormal enlargement of the prostate gland.
Collection of fluid resembling a true cyst but lacking an epithelial lining; common in pancreatitis.
Bacterial skin infection producing pus-filled lesions.
Elevated body temperature above normal range; a clinical term for fever.
Substance that induces fever by acting on the hypothalamic thermoregulatory center.
Presence of white blood cells in urine; indicates urinary tract infection or inflammation.
Complete paralysis of all four limbs due to high cervical spinal cord injury.
Protrusion of the rectum through the anal opening.
Herniation of the posterior vaginal wall with protrusion of the rectum.
Return or worsening of a disease after a period of improvement or remission.
Surgical removal of all or part of an organ or tissue.
Separation of the retina from the underlying retinal pigment epithelium.
Non-inflammatory disease of the retina; most commonly diabetic or hypertensive.
Bloodstream infection with proliferating bacteria; a clinical term largely replaced by sepsis.
Excessive drowsiness or sleepiness; may indicate CNS depression or metabolic disorder.
Device used to measure arterial blood pressure.
Pulmonary function test measuring the volume and flow of air inhaled and exhaled.
Forward slipping of one vertebra over the one below, causing back pain and nerve compression.
Junction between two neurons where nerve impulses are transmitted chemically or electrically.
Viscous lubricating fluid secreted by the synovial membrane within joint cavities.
Dilation of small superficial blood vessels forming visible red lines on the skin.
Surgical anchoring of a tendon to bone to restore function or stability.
Instrument for measuring body temperature.
Pharmacological dissolution of a blood clot using thrombolytic agents.
Instrument used to measure intraocular pressure for glaucoma screening.
Presence of bacterial toxins or metabolic waste products in the bloodstream.
Relating to drug administration through the skin for systemic effect.
Transfer of blood or blood components from a donor into a recipient's bloodstream.
Having three cusps; refers to the tricuspid valve between the right atrium and ventricle.
Each of the three successive three-month periods of a pregnancy.
Diagnostic test measuring the movement of the tympanic membrane in response to air pressure.
Protrusion of abdominal contents through a defect at the navel.
Presence of calculi (stones) within the ureter.
Formation of stones within the urinary tract.
Abnormal enlargement of the pampiniform venous plexus within the scrotum.
Puncture of a vein with a needle to obtain blood or administer treatment.
Life-threatening arrhythmia with chaotic ventricular electrical activity and no effective output.
Abnormal backflow of urine from the bladder into the ureters.
Abnormal enlargement of one or more abdominal organs.
Yellow discoloration of the skin, often from carotenemia or jaundice.
Yellow fatty deposit under the skin caused by elevated blood lipids.
Presence of albumin protein in the urine; an early marker of kidney damage.
Archaic term for iron-deficiency anemia causing greenish-yellow skin pallor.
Light-sensitive pigment in retinal rod cells essential for vision in low-light conditions.
Adjacent to the glomerulus; juxtaglomerular cells regulate blood pressure via renin secretion.
The largest of the eight carpal bones, located at the center of the wrist.
Study of biological rhythms and their effects on physiology, behavior, and health.
Pertaining to the visceral organs of the abdomen; splanchnic nerves supply the abdominal organs.
Normal left-sided positioning of the heart; the term is used when situs inversus affects other organs.
Congenital condition where the heart is positioned on the right side of the chest.
Describing any procedure or drug delivery method that passes through the skin rather than through a natural body opening.
A refractive error caused by an irregularly shaped cornea or lens, preventing light from focusing evenly on the retina and causing blurred or distorted vision at any distance.
A ring of smooth muscle at the junction of the stomach and duodenum that regulates the passage of partially digested food into the small intestine.
The lower esophageal sphincter located at the junction of the esophagus and stomach; prevents reflux of gastric contents into the esophagus.
A hormone secreted by the pineal gland that regulates the sleep-wake cycle in response to light and darkness.
A posterior pituitary hormone that promotes water reabsorption in the renal collecting ducts, reducing urine output and regulating plasma osmolality; also called vasopressin.
A major peripheral nerve of the upper limb supplying sensation to the palm and motor function to thenar muscles; compressed in carpal tunnel syndrome.
Any substance, typically a protein, that the immune system recognizes as foreign and mounts a specific immune response against.
A protein (immunoglobulin) produced by B lymphocytes that binds specifically to an antigen to neutralize it or mark it for destruction.
The lobe of the cerebral cortex located posterior to the frontal lobe; processes sensory information including touch, pressure, temperature, and spatial awareness.
A small endocrine gland in the brain that secretes melatonin, regulating circadian rhythms and sleep-wake cycles.
The mature female reproductive cell (gamete) released from the ovary during ovulation; if fertilized by a sperm, it develops into an embryo.
Small anatomical sac; ovarian follicles are fluid-filled structures containing an immature egg; skin follicles are tubular structures from which hairs grow.
One of the paired male gonads located in the scrotum; produces sperm and testosterone.
The muscular duct that transports sperm from the epididymis to the ejaculatory duct during ejaculation.
A class of drugs that reduce pain, fever, and inflammation by inhibiting cyclooxygenase (COX) enzymes; includes ibuprofen, naproxen, and aspirin.
A condition in which a person believes they are pregnant and experiences physical symptoms of pregnancy — including abdominal distension and amenorrhea — without an actual fetus.
The largest and uppermost bone of the pelvis, forming the prominent hip structure palpable on each side of the body.
A digital version of a patient's medical history, diagnoses, medications, and treatment records, maintained by a healthcare provider.
Medication available for purchase without a prescription; used for self-treatment of minor conditions. Contrast: prescription medicine, which requires a physician's authorization.
Medication requiring a physician's written authorization before dispensing; for conditions needing medical supervision. Contrast: OTC medicine.
One of the paired ducts that extend from the uterus toward each ovary; the site of fertilization and the pathway for the ovum to reach the uterus.
The muscular canal extending from the cervix to the external genitalia; serves as the birth canal during delivery and the passageway for menstrual flow.
The collective term for the external female genitalia, including the labia majora, labia minora, clitoris, and vaginal opening.
A coiled tube located on the posterior surface of each testis where sperm mature and are stored before ejaculation.
The male external reproductive and urinary organ through which urine and semen are expelled from the body.
One of a pair of glands located posterior to the bladder that secretes fructose-rich fluid contributing to seminal fluid volume.
An anatomical plane that divides the body into left and right portions; the midsagittal (median) plane passes through the midline.
An anatomical plane that divides the body into anterior and posterior portions; also called the coronal plane.
An anatomical plane that divides the body into superior and inferior portions; the basis of CT scan cross-sectional imaging.
The anatomical region corresponding to the upper arm, between the shoulder and elbow.
The anatomical region pertaining to the head.
The anatomical region pertaining to the neck; also refers to the cervical spine (C1–C7 vertebrae).
The anatomical region corresponding to the leg (shin), between the knee and ankle.
The bony cavity formed by the skull that houses and protects the brain and its coverings (meninges).
The canal formed by the vertebral column that encloses and protects the spinal cord.
The body cavity enclosed by the rib cage and diaphragm, containing the heart, lungs, and major vessels.
The combined abdominal and pelvic cavities, separated from the thoracic cavity by the diaphragm; contains digestive, reproductive, and urinary organs.
The serous membrane lining the abdominal cavity and covering most abdominal organs; inflammation constitutes peritonitis.
One of four clinical abdominal regions — RUQ, RLQ, LUQ, LLQ — used to localize pain, organs, and examination findings.
One of nine clinical abdominal divisions: right/left hypochondriac, lumbar, inguinal; and central epigastric, umbilical, and hypogastric regions.
Directional term indicating a position closer to the midline of the body.
Directional term indicating a position farther from the midline of the body.
Directional term indicating a position closer to the point of attachment or origin of a limb.
Directional term indicating a position farther from the point of attachment or origin of a limb.
The tip or pointed end of a structure; clinically used for the apex of the heart (pointing left and downward) and the apex of the lung (superior tip).
The deepest layer of the epidermis where keratinocytes actively divide and melanocytes reside; responsible for continuous skin cell renewal.
A pigment-producing cell found in the basal layer of the epidermis that synthesizes melanin, which gives skin its color and provides protection against UV radiation.
A tube-like structure in the skin from which a hair grows; associated with sebaceous glands and arrector pili muscles.
An exocrine gland in the skin that produces sweat; classified as eccrine (found throughout the body) or apocrine (found in axillary and groin areas).
A gland in the skin that secretes sebum into hair follicles to lubricate and waterproof the skin and hair.
Specialized nerve endings in the skin and other tissues that detect stimuli such as touch, pressure, pain, temperature, and vibration.
A hard keratinized plate covering the dorsal surface of the distal phalanges; protects fingertips and aids fine manipulation.
The pale, crescent-shaped area at the base of a nail, representing the visible portion of the nail matrix.
The thin fold of skin at the base of the nail that seals and protects the nail matrix from infection.
The oily substance secreted by sebaceous glands that lubricates the skin and hair and has mild antimicrobial properties.
A chronic inflammatory condition of the pilosebaceous unit, characterized by comedones, papules, pustules, and nodules; most common in adolescents.
Pertaining to or affecting the skin.
A superficial wound caused by friction that removes the outer layers of skin without penetrating the dermis.
A fluid-filled skin lesion greater than 1 cm in diameter; seen in burns, pemphigus, and severe contact dermatitis.
A plugged hair follicle filled with sebum and dead skin cells; open comedones appear as blackheads, closed comedones as whiteheads.
An injury to soft tissue causing hemorrhage beneath the intact skin, resulting in discoloration and tenderness; commonly called a bruise.
A closed, epithelium-lined sac filled with fluid, semifluid, or solid material; may be benign or malignant.
Partial or complete loss of skin pigment (melanin), resulting in lighter or white patches.
Profuse, abnormal sweating occurring without physical exertion; may indicate fever, shock, hypoglycemia, or autonomic dysfunction.
Widespread redness and scaling involving most of the body surface area; a severe inflammatory skin condition also known as exfoliative dermatitis.
A dry, leathery crust of dead tissue formed over a wound or burn; must be removed (debrided) to allow healing.
A narrow linear crack or tear in the skin surface extending into the dermis; commonly seen on the heels, fingertips, and lips.
An excess of blood in a tissue or organ, resulting in redness and warmth; may be active (increased arterial flow) or passive (venous congestion).
Darkening of skin areas due to excess melanin production; causes include sun exposure, hormonal changes, and inflammation.
Any abnormal change in tissue structure caused by disease or injury; a general term encompassing wounds, sores, tumors, and rashes.
A condition of localized loss of skin pigmentation, resulting in white patches; may be idiopathic or associated with systemic conditions.
A benign proliferation of melanocytes forming a pigmented skin lesion; may become malignant (melanoma) if certain changes occur.
A solid, raised skin lesion greater than 1 cm in diameter, extending deeper into the dermis or subcutaneous tissue than a papule.
Abnormal softening of the nails, often associated with nutritional deficiency or chemical exposure.
Abnormal paleness of the skin, mucous membranes, or nail beds, often indicating anemia, shock, or poor perfusion.
A small, solid, raised skin lesion less than 1 cm in diameter without visible fluid; seen in acne, eczema, and psoriasis.
Abnormal or exaggerated skin reaction to sunlight or UV radiation; may be drug-induced or associated with autoimmune conditions such as SLE.
Containing, consisting of, or forming pus; indicates active bacterial infection.
A small, raised skin lesion filled with pus; common in acne, folliculitis, and impetigo.
A chronic autoimmune connective tissue disease causing progressive fibrosis and hardening of the skin and internal organs; formerly called scleroderma.
Excessive secretion of sebum from the sebaceous glands, resulting in oily skin and scalp; associated with seborrheic dermatitis.
Characterized by the production of pus; describing an infection involving pus formation.
An open sore on the skin or mucous membrane resulting from tissue breakdown; may be caused by infection, pressure, ischemia, or inflammation.
A small, fluid-filled skin lesion less than 1 cm in diameter; seen in herpes, chickenpox, and contact dermatitis.
A transient, raised, pale skin lesion surrounded by erythema caused by localized edema in the dermis; the primary lesion of urticaria.
Abnormal dryness of the skin due to decreased sebum production or impaired skin barrier function; also called xerosis cutis.
A localized collection of pus within a cavity formed by tissue breakdown, surrounded by inflamed tissue; requires drainage.
A chronic inflammatory skin condition affecting the central face, characterized by persistent redness, visible blood vessels, and acne-like papules and pustules.
The most common form of acne, involving comedones, papules, pustules, and nodules due to sebaceous gland inflammation and Cutibacterium acnes.
A congenital absence or deficiency of melanin pigment in the skin, hair, and eyes due to genetic defects in melanin synthesis.
The most common skin cancer, arising from basal cells of the epidermis; locally invasive but rarely metastasizes; typically caused by chronic UV exposure.
Tissue injury caused by heat, chemicals, electricity, or radiation; classified by depth (first, second, third degree) and extent of body surface involved.
A scar formed by fibrous connective tissue replacing normal skin after wound healing.
A localized injury to skin and underlying tissue resulting from prolonged pressure, typically over bony prominences; also called a pressure ulcer or bedsore.
A general term for any non-inflammatory skin disease or disorder.
Death of body tissue due to loss of blood supply or severe bacterial infection; classified as dry (ischemic) or wet (infectious).
Ischemic necrosis of tissue without bacterial infection, typically in the extremities; the tissue desiccates and mummifies.
Necrosis of tissue complicated by bacterial infection, producing a moist, malodorous wound with rapid tissue destruction.
Chronic inflammatory skin condition causing itching, redness, and a scaly rash; often used interchangeably with atopic dermatitis.
A group of genetic skin disorders characterized by dry, thickened, scaly skin resembling fish scales; ranges from mild to severe.
Highly contagious superficial bacterial skin infection in children, caused by Staphylococcus aureus or Streptococcus pyogenes; presents with honey-colored crusted lesions.
A malignant vascular tumor of the skin and mucous membranes caused by human herpesvirus 8 (HHV-8); strongly associated with HIV/AIDS.
An overgrowth of fibrous scar tissue beyond the original wound margins; more common in darker-skinned individuals and may cause pruritus or pain.
Abnormal thickening of the outer layer of skin (keratin); includes actinic keratosis (precancerous) and seborrheic keratosis (benign).
An irregular tear or cut in the skin or soft tissue, typically caused by trauma; usually requires wound closure.
An aggressive cancer arising from melanocytes; the most dangerous form of skin cancer due to its high propensity for metastasis.
Infestation of the skin or hair by parasitic lice (Pediculus species); causes intense itching and may be transmitted through close contact.
A contagious viral infection characterized by a fine pink rash; dangerous in pregnancy as it causes congenital rubella syndrome in the fetus.
A contagious skin infestation by the mite Sarcoptes scabiei, causing intense nocturnal itching and a papular rash, particularly in skin folds.
A benign, slow-growing cyst beneath the skin formed by the accumulation of keratin and sebum within a follicular sac.
A malignant tumor arising from squamous (keratinocyte) cells of the epidermis; may metastasize if untreated; associated with UV exposure and immunosuppression.
A benign vascular birthmark composed of proliferating blood vessels; appears in infancy and typically involutes spontaneously by early childhood.
A chronic systemic autoimmune disease affecting multiple organs; characterized by a butterfly-shaped facial rash, joint pain, and renal involvement.
A superficial fungal infection of the skin, hair, or nails caused by dermatophytes; named by body site (e.g., tinea capitis — scalp, tinea pedis — foot).
Dermatophyte fungal infection of the scalp and hair shafts; predominantly affects children and may cause hair loss.
A common dermatophyte infection of the feet, particularly between the toes; characterized by itching, scaling, and fissures.
A highly contagious primary infection with varicella-zoster virus, presenting with a generalized itchy vesicular rash; can cause serious complications in immunocompromised patients.
A benign epidermal growth caused by human papillomavirus (HPV) infection; types include verruca vulgaris (common wart) and verruca plantaris (plantar wart).
An autoimmune condition causing progressive depigmentation of the skin in irregular patches due to destruction of melanocytes.
A deep skin infection involving a cluster of interconnected furuncles, typically caused by Staphylococcus aureus; produces a large, painful, pus-filled mass.
A deep bacterial infection of a hair follicle and surrounding tissue, forming a painful, pus-filled nodule; caused by Staphylococcus aureus.
A bacterial infection of the hair shafts in axillary or pubic regions, caused by Corynebacterium species; characterized by yellowish, reddish, or black concretions on hair.
Inflammation of the nail matrix, often causing pain, swelling, and nail deformity.
Compulsive biting or chewing of the nails; may be habitual or associated with anxiety disorders.
Infection of the skin surrounding the nail, causing redness, swelling, and pus; may be acute (bacterial) or chronic (fungal).
A laboratory test in which a specimen is cultured to identify the causative microorganism and then tested to determine which antibiotics it is susceptible to.
Surgical removal of an entire lesion along with a margin of surrounding tissue for histopathological examination.
Microscopic examination of cells shed from a body surface or obtained by scraping; used to detect malignancy or infection.
A rapid intraoperative histological technique in which tissue is snap-frozen, sectioned, and examined microscopically to guide surgical decisions.
A diagnostic procedure in which skin, nail, or hair samples are scraped and examined microscopically or cultured to identify fungal infection.
A tissue graft transplanted between genetically non-identical individuals of the same species; used as a temporary biological wound covering.
A tissue graft harvested from and transplanted to the same individual; the gold standard for permanent skin replacement.
A surgical instrument used to cut thin slices of skin for grafting; also refers to the area of skin innervated by a single spinal nerve root.
Surgical repair or reconstruction of the skin, typically using grafting techniques.
Transplantation of healthy skin to cover a wound where skin has been lost due to burns, trauma, or surgery.
A tissue graft taken from a donor of a different species (e.g., porcine skin); used as a temporary wound covering.
Destruction of tissue using heat, cold, electricity, or chemicals to stop bleeding, remove lesions, or prevent infection.
Destruction of abnormal tissue by application of extreme cold, typically using liquid nitrogen; used for warts, actinic keratoses, and skin cancers.
Removal of tissue from a body cavity or surface using a curette (spoon-shaped instrument); used to sample or remove skin lesions.
Use of a high-frequency electric current to cut tissue or coagulate blood vessels to control bleeding during surgery.
A minor surgical procedure to open and drain a skin abscess or infected cyst, relieving pain and facilitating healing.
Surgical removal of all or part of a nail; performed for severe infection, ingrown nail, or tumor.
Application of a chemical solution to the skin to remove the outer layers, promoting resurfacing; used for acne scars, fine lines, and pigmentation.
Mechanical resurfacing of the skin using a rotating abrasive instrument to remove superficial layers; used for scars, wrinkles, and tattoo removal.
Use of focused light energy to treat skin conditions including vascular lesions, pigmentation, scars, hair removal, and skin resurfacing.
A surgical procedure that removes subcutaneous fat deposits by suction through small incisions; used for body contouring.
Surgical removal of excess facial skin and tightening of underlying tissues to reduce the appearance of wrinkles and facial sagging.
An agent that produces loss of sensation with or without loss of consciousness; classified as local (affecting a specific area) or general (affecting the whole body).
A drug that kills or inhibits the growth of fungi; used to treat infections such as tinea, candidiasis, and onychomycosis.
A drug that acts against parasites including protozoa, helminths, and ectoparasites; used to treat conditions such as scabies, pediculosis, and malaria.
An agent that relieves or prevents itching; includes antihistamines, topical corticosteroids, and calamine lotion.
A substance applied to living tissue to prevent or inhibit the growth of microorganisms and reduce the risk of infection.
A topical preparation containing a corticosteroid to reduce skin inflammation, itching, and redness; used in eczema, psoriasis, and contact dermatitis.
